results.title: Why are AI agents frustrating? Exosphere solves the problem. | Nikita Agarwal posted on the topic | LinkedIn
results.content:
AI agents are mostly bullsh\t. I've built 70+ of them here are 3 that actually work. MIT just released a report: 95% of AI projects fail. After deploying dozens of these systems, I know exactly why. Everyone's chasing flashy agent "swarms" instead of building things that work. Companies fall for complex multi-agent systems that sound great in demos but break in real life. Here's what happens: one agent makes stuff up, and your entire system fails. AI will confidently lie rather than say "I don't know." (I've watched a client's "revolutionary" 10-agent system crash because we got caught up in the hype.) The real damage?  Burned budgets with zero results  Stakeholders who lose trust in AI  Teams wasting months on systems that never work  Joining that 95% failure rate But three [...] Why is everyone (including me) crazy about AI agents today? Agency. But if agents are taking away from your agency instead of multiplying it, what's the point? Spending hours setting them up. Spending hours refining the prompts. Spending hours deploying them. What's the point of agents then? -'I would rather do it myself!' Which is why everyone is (borderline) frustrated by how agents are performing today. We need proof of true agency. And for such proof, agents need STRUCTURE. To reliably build trust. They still need someone to hold them and instruct which pathways to take and avoid. That's Exosphere. An AI workflow engine that can run smallest tasks to create largest workflows, up and running in under 30 mins with your custom code. With validations, structure, and the flexibility to [...] + Report this post

results.title: Why AI agent teams often fail to work together
results.content:
There are serious, successful AI agent teams. For such a team, the difficulty of a task doesn’t really matter that much. What matters is whether the task can be broken down into separate parts that don’t depend on each other, according to the Google DeepMind paper. The researchers called this “decomposability.”

A financial analyst, for example, has to review a lot of information from separate sources, such as news reports, SEC filings and business records. Several AI agents can do these tasks in parallel more efficiently than one agent doing them in turn, the researchers found. [...] “In many settings, the current AI agents do not actually work very well as a team,” says computer scientist James Zou of Stanford University. He has done extensive work with agents, including running the first scientific meeting for AI-led research.

Research backs the observations. Late last year, Google DeepMind researchers posted a paper to arXiv.org about bot teams. The study, which has yet to go through peer review, suggests that a team of AI agents often performs worse than a single agent working alone.

Seems counterintuitive, right?

To make sure we’re ready for the workplaces, social networks and labs of the future, we need to better understand the weird and wild world of AI agent teams — where they fail and, surprisingly, where they thrive. Here are three examples. [...] This is a human-written story voiced by AI. Got feedback?  Take our survey . (See our AI policy  here .)

OpenAI’s ChatGPT or Anthropic’s Claude regularly answer our questions. And souped-up versions of these chatbots, called AI agents, take actions on their own, helping people with appointments, coding and more. AI agents are starting to contribute to science and finance, often working together in carefully organized teams.  
  
In the business world, endless webinars and guides explain how to welcome AI agents into a workplace. Most of this material focuses on how people can work effectively with AI agents. But as these bots become more common and more capable, they’ll also have to work well with each other.

And so far, experiments into bot teamwork have revealed some serious flaws.

results.title: Why Enterprise AI Agents are Currently Broken (And How to Fix It)
results.content:
So, if you are running autonomous AI agents, you might be not might be for sure be sitting on massive security and financial time bomb without even realizing it. You know, lately the developer community has been obsessed with MCP as we all know. It's a universal standard that lets you plug any AI model directly into your internal databases, your local files, and your APIs. But while everyone is having fun tinkering with these agents on their laptops, scaling them into the real team introduces two massive production challenges. The first one being uh quite literally devastating security risks, and the second one being the um runaway API bills. # Issue: Custom Agent works on Web but has problems in Microsoft Teams

Erik Duong    0 Reputation points

Hi everyone,

I’m currently deploying an internal MSB Research Agent (custom AI agent) for employees in my organisation.

✅ On web (browser): everything works normally ⚠️ On Microsoft Teams: we are experiencing inconsistent issues

### ❗ Problems observed in Teams

 Agent sometimes fails to load
 Responses are not returned or get stuck
 Authentication/session seems unstable in some cases

### 📌 Context [...] ✅ On web (browser): everything works normally   
 ⚠️ On Microsoft Teams: we are experiencing inconsistent issues

### ❗ Problems observed in Teams

 Agent sometimes fails to load
 Responses are not returned or get stuck
 Authentication/session seems unstable in some cases

### 📌 Context

 The same agent and backend works fine on web
 Issues only appear when accessed via Teams (app / embedded experience)
 This is impacting user adoption significantly as most employees use Teams as their primary workspace

### 🙏 Looking for advice

Has anyone experienced similar issues when:

 Integrating custom agents / bots into Teams?
 Deploying AI copilots inside Teams vs web?

Any guidance on: [...] ### 📌 Context

 The same agent and backend works fine on web

 This is impacting user adoption significantly as most employees use Teams as their primary workspace
